summary: This podcast transcript covers the story of Stormpulse, a bootstrapped weather risk management SaaS that grew from a side project to being used in the White House Situation Room. Patrick McKenzie interviews founder Matt Wensing about the company's transition from freemium (6.5M free users) to a paid B2B model, their experience raising angel funding outside Silicon Valley, and the mechanics of selling enterprise software. The conversation covers convertible notes, the role of social proof in fundraising, why most people shouldn't angel invest, and how founders have unique advantages in enterprise sales. McKenzie and Wensing both emphasize that B2B software is chronically underpriced and that founders should charge far more than their instincts suggest.
key_points: Stormpulse bootstrapped to White House adoption through product quality alone — the Situation Room signed up via self-serve, proving that great products can sell themselves even to the most demanding customers

Fundraising follows a social proof cascade: Matt spoke to ~80 people, got almost all no's early, then yes's accelerated as momentum built — described in 65 characters as 'NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNYNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNYNNNNYNNYYYYYYYYYYY'
Traction means evidence you can capture value at a rate that moves the business forward — 6.5M free users didn't count as traction until they converted to ~1,000 paying customers
Angel investing has terrible expected returns for most people; the median VC return is probably negative, and amateur investors picking from non-cream-of-the-crop companies will fare worse
Founders have an enormous advantage in enterprise sales because they can speak with authority about both business and technical details, making customers feel they're getting special attention
money_quotes: My definition of traction, if I can throw it out there, is evidence that you can capture value at a rate that moves the business forward.
I want to disabuse you from that notion, right now. For the vast majority of people listening to this, angel investment is not a good idea.
Most people will get wiped out totally.
One takeaway from this podcast, for anybody attempting to run a business on $3.95 a month, don't.
The one positive thing that I'll say about angel investing is at least it's better than Bitcoin.
No is the default, it has to be.
If KissMetrics costs $150 a month, it's done. If it increases sales by five percent over the year, it pays for itself in perpetuity.
The first time I got a check from somebody that was a real company, like a Fortune 500 company, paying an invoice that I had just made up was like this moment of clarity.
key_insight: Traction isn't vanity metrics — it's evidence you can capture value at an accelerating rate, and the path from bootstrapping to funded growth requires mastering social proof both for fundraising and enterprise sales.
